commentBody:
I like this one a lot. But I think there was a lot of wheelhouse in it for me. Everything fell pretty quickly. I struggled a tad with the northwest.

Objectively I hope people find it a good Saturday, but I get a sense people will consider Friday’s puzzle a bit harder. 

The long entries, specifically, the down, long entries,were fun and life imitates art was amazing. 

I feel like so many different knowledge bases were required to get this puzzle quickly so I feel like it was a pretty gratifying solve for me personally but I am curious to see of course what the mob thinks about this puzzle.

In any case. Kareem.  I am a fan. Thank you

Seems like it's been a long time since I haf a Saturday PB, but this one hit square in my wheelhouse. Got 1A immediately and just rolled from there, despite a brief interruption and the constant booming of fireworks in my neighborhood. Too bad my dog wasn't here to celebrate with me; she's too busy hiding under the bed!

I just had to drop a comment in here, as this is the first time I've ever solved the puzzle at drop time - I usually go to bed at 9:00. But everyone's shooting off fireworks tonight, so I'm not going to bother trying to sleep. 
It was a pretty easy puzzle for me, just hit the groove and sailed right through it I guess.
The fireworks are dying down, so I guess I'll go to bed. Good night!

You know how sometimes a whole row tells a little story? That happened to me in the bottom rows.

ESTEE PALM PILOT: Ms. Lauder was ahead of her time, in offering a special gold branded one for sale.

STERN CREAM SODA: it cuts way back on the sugar, vanilla, and fizz. It’s plain water.

TERSE STONE TOOL: Barry’s trusty chisel has failed to enter more than three answers; he had to finish up with a pencil.
Fun but way too easy for a Saturday!

I loved the clues in this puzzle.  While they weren’t outrageously obscure, a high percentage of them were playful enough to give me pause, GODHELPME.  I liked “where students learn about congress,” and was fortunate that civics class didn’t fit.  Of course I’m dating myself, as I fear civics class is but a dusty memory.  I did hit on SEXEDCLASS pretty quickly, as I got the X from TELEX (another dim and fading memory), and I recalled a clue from an earlier puzzle that also played off the apolitical meaning of congress.  I actually thought playground would be a more accurate answer.

The puzzle’s timely mention of BIKERACE was also a reminder that, with the Tour de France starting tomorrow, and with the World Cup in the knockout round, I’m about to hit maximum sports overload.  I’m sure that cycling mad France is excited about the emergence of a brilliant 20 year old rider, Paul Seixas, who carries the promise and the burden of returning cycling glory to France. Could a World Cup/Tour de France sweep be in the cards.  Probably not.

You know those bar fights where everyone keeps whacking each other with their hats? CAPBATTLES? That's gotta be a thing, somewhere ... just not in today's grid 😀 

Thanks for the fun crossword, Kareem!
I suppose in the descriptor "fairly challenging grid," the emphasis is on "fairly." I was three seconds off my personal best solve time.

Oh, oh, oh...very tricky.

I technically got it, but I really didn't.  Let me explain.

I finished the puzzle with a lot of confidence, but got the "Almost" screen.  I flyspecked and flyspecked and flyspecked and could not find the error.  

Eventually I had to look up 6D.  That's when I found out that it was SCHEMA, which I have heard of and have used quite often in database design, instead of SCHEMe.  

TARANA/SCHEMA cross.  That, I feel, was pretty tough.

So for my money this puzzle was *not* easy, because I needed a lookup.  And virtually the entire reason I needed a lookup was because the proper name crossing a word which could really end in an E or an A.

Bummer.  I want my money back.  The editors were terrible.  The constructor was terrible.  Everybody who got this is terrible.  The world is terrible.  Space is terrible.  Saturn is terrible.  ANDROMEDA is terrible.  Excuse me while I go shoot a TAPIR or two.

This comment section is going to be terrible, because I'm going to throw a fit over and over.

🤬🤬🤬🤬🤬

Everybody hates me.  I'm going to go eat some worms.

Appropriate that we get a banger on the 4th of July! Knew I was in for a fun solve when I saw Kareem Ayas's name and was not disappointed. It took me a thoroughly enjoyable 10:16 to get the Gold Star.

Certain Ivy Leaguer:

In these puzzles you can be certain that the Ivy Leaguer is an ELI.

Fun Saturday today.  I get Saturdays maybe a third of the time and got it today.

I enjoyed the spanner LIFE IMITATES ART a lot.  Southeast corner with CREAM SODA, PALM PILOT, SOULMATE was also fun.

Northwest was the toughest to me.  Just because I haven't heard of TARANA Burke or OCEANARIA, or the movie BACKDRAFT and felt BIKE RACE and RAP BATTLE were especially indirect clues.

Overall a lot of very good cluing, the sort of clues that make no sense at first then make sense when you see them in a different light.
Nice quiet animation on the MIdi.
Much easier puzzle than Thursday or Friday.  My turn to say it was a personal best time in  15 months of NYT Saturday puzzles.
BACKDRAFT was a gimme for me.  I saw the movie starring Kurt Russell, and then the LA  Universal theme park had an "experience" ("ride" is the wrong word) based on it, which  I saw in 1994.

Very benign, and a personal best for a Saturday (nothing to brag of, my times usually pretty ordinary compared with those claimed by many commentators).

Happy 4th of July and 250th Anniversary of Independence for those US citizens who might be reading this!
For the mini: 4 Down is a MAJOR spoiler for a film that came out last year!!! If you haven’t done the mini yet, you’ve been WARNED’

Took a few passes but it all fell into place.  Loved this theme less.  Just enough gimmes to start solving the rest. 

Kelsey Pfendler will land in Honolulu in about three hours.  She’s crushed all of the solo records for unsupported row from Monterrey to Honolulu. 

Happy 4th!

I done did this pandering puzz.

A Saturday that I finish in 15 minutes is a Wednesday

And absolutely not Kareem's fault.  By now I am well aware of his talent for delight and deception from numerous past grids which vexed me no end before yielding their gold.

Loyalist editors in thrall to their monarchic masters on this Independence Day.
A nice Tuesday puzzle - I finished in less than 12 minutes, which is pretty much my Tuesday average. How did this run on Saturday?
Oof, big movie spoiler in the mini.
It is time to stop using the Christian-defined term "Old Testament" and instead use the neutral "Hebrew Bible".

Excuse me for bringing up this topic for the billionth time, but I think this puzzle is a great example of the problems of assigning a global difficulty to a puzzle.

Matt from "Spring Hill" and Andrzej from ... well, Andrzej doesn't need any introduction, both considered this is very easy puzzle.  Quarter hour solve.  Fine, I really have no argument with that.

Had I know Tarana Burke, which is on me that I didn't, but if I had....or....if I decided to do a lookup there because it's dead simple with Google, then I would have gone on and though the puzzle was really quite easy.

But I didn't so I had an error I would never find.

So the same puzzle, for me it's a monster streak-ender, for them it was a Wednesday stroll in the park.
Certainly the easiest Saturday puzzle in a while.

I had Big Touble in Little China. Whoops, that's a different Kurt Russell movie.  BACKDRAFT was a blockbuster? Okay then.    In the NW, I cycled through the 4-lettered Starks, Arya (It's always Arya! Hi, Nancy), then Nedd* and finally ROBB. But I also misread 4 Down as [Small snack] instead of [Small smack] so entered Nosh. D'oh. A lot of ripping out and staring at that corner.  
Anyway, the rest of the puzzle filled in pretty smoothly for a Saturday and on the whole i enjoyed solving it.

*not sure if it's spelled with two Ds, but I tried it.
As someone who has participated in rap battles, love the clue!

On the easy side as I missed only 2 or 3 letters as I never go to bars unless they're wine bars, never watched Game of Thrones as too much sex and nudity alongside extreme depictions of violence - instead I try to solve crosswords  which may or may not involve any of that - and never knew until now the plural of oceanarium.

A leisurely puzzle and lucky for me. A Bourne movie in the background and fireworks booming outside.  I don't keep time anymore, but it was pretty quick for a Saturday.

Tomorrow is our country's 250th, and next month is my 92nd.  I don't quite know how to think about that and don't relate to it much.
This was easier than Thursday's puzzle.

A fun grid that didn’t put up much resistance to me (and apparently others)—it didn’t hurt that BACKDRAFT, ANDROMEDA and GOLDENCALF were gimmes to me as clued. Definitely a solid grid though without a lot of glue, and I enjoyed learning the Oscar Wilde quote.

Coincidentally, I just finished watching Olivia Wilde’s latest fantastic movie “The Invite” which I highly recommend, and also began with an Oscar Wilde quote: "One should always be in love. That is the reason one should never marry."

I guess I’m too hung up on Jane Austen and E.M. Forster,  but I connect the Grand Tour to the long trip to the continent that young rich Britishers always used to take as a rite of passage. Happy to learn it’s also a BIKERACE.

Do am I to understand TRUMAN married his Bess friend?

Not much else struck me today. Happy Birthday America; and happy birthday Rasa. If by tomorrow you mean Sunday, then we share a birthday, so astrologically speaking that must make you pretty darn cool.

TAPIRS gives me a chance to post a link to “Young Tapir” by one of my favorite living artists, Kyoko Imazu, an artist who imitates and pays loving respect to life: https://kyokoimazu.com/artwork/2806800-Young%20Malay%20Tapir.html As @vaer might say, any puzzle with CREAM SODA is okay by me. Happy 4th to those of you who observe.

Loved the graphics in the Midi 🎆🎇 Really enjoyed today’s puzzle, Kareem. It felt both fresh and nostalgic at the same time. Our neighbor, Jeff Davis, is in the midst of the Tour 21 BIKE RACE to raise funds for Cure Leukaemia. He is currently riding all 21 stages of the Tour de France. You can follow along on Instagram https://www.instagram.com/jeff_w_davis Happy 250th to USA.

This may offend some, but be that as it may. If you comment, ‘the puzzle was too easy—I only needed X look-ups’ then you didn’t find the puzzle easy—you found it unsolvable.

Yes, some of those look ups will be trivia, to be fair, but trivia is part of crosswords.

Perhaps I’m just grumpy because this took me a respectable 40 minutes. They were enjoyable minutes, nonetheless.

Pedantry requires to note that there are no major or minor galaxies. You can have dwarfs, ultracompact dwarfs, elliptical, spiral, lenticular, barred, starbursts, BCGs, … and many others but not major ones. cluing ANDROMEDA as our nearest neighbour that isn’t named after Magellan would have been better. 

The clue is wrong — waiting for the usual suspects to excuse the editing and/or proofreading teams, — by saying that Wikipedia describes it do. :) 

Nice puzzle. I don’t get the fuss about SCHEMA crossed with a proper name. Perfectly legal misdirection. 

Happy Birthday US Americans. Hope you restore yourselves. A restorative period of calm democracy may help.

TIL 20A VENDEE is someone who buys; not someone who sells.

Whadda ya know?

Happy Fourth of July to all you American solvers! Wishing you a great CCL! 

I must say, i sometimes joke about the seniority of the NYT solvers, and i can’t help but notice that many of these clues would have stumped our children’s generation:

What’s a fax?
What’s a PDA?
What’s a beatnik?

Dear Mr Ayas,  you are spoiling us! Medieval Latin hymns, PresidentTruman, Maureen O’Hara and Oscar Wilde hit us right in the demographic sweet spot. 

But ending on STONE TOOL? Steady on, Mr Ayas, sir! Senior, elderly, geriatric, that’s us! Now, you want to go Paleolithic? Noli me tangere! Get thee to the Bridge club!

From The Guardian - 48 photographs that capture America at 250: https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2026/jul/04/america-250-anniversary-photos-civil-rights-moon-landing-9-11-gold-rush?CMP=Share_AndroidApp_Other

I was pretty pleased with myself because even though I found this one challenging, I managed to finish it. Not quickly, but with no lookups and minimal changes. All while balancing the needs of two noise-phobic dogs in full firework panic mode. (A mere warmup for tomorrow, I fear.)

And then I come here to a chorus of “too easy”s. “Like a Tuesday!” “Personal best!” “Solved it with my eyes closed!” (OK, I made the last one up, but still.) Knock it off, gloaters. You’re killing my good vibes.

When solving in the app I have found that recently there’s a slight delay between completing the solve and getting the happy music. The same has always been the case for the Midi but only recently for the full crossword. It’s not a big thing but I wish the NYT app developers would listen to our feedback. The Wordplay column also used to open in the browser rather than the Games app itself making it easier to switch back and forth if you needed help with a solve.

and perhaps most annoying, responding to the clue at 28a "Follower of X or G" with a confident MAN, since "follower" was singular not plural?

So I am part of the chorus that will say this was too easy for a Saturday. And in anticipation of commenters who get cross when people say that (why? because they want to feel good about solving the NYXW on Saturday no matter how dumbed down it is?) here is a raft of examples of cluing that was WAY too easy for Saturday:

12D [False idol in the Old Testament] — One of several literal clues. Anyone who is passingly familiar with the Bible — which should be a basic prerequisite for a Saturday crossword — knows this instantly. There’s not even an attempt at cleverness. 

13D [Army specialist with a headwear-inspired nickname] — Are you kidding me? Why not just add to the clue “Starts with G, B” or “Rhymes with Clean Hurray”?

51A [“The One”] — Surely there is a tricky/clever play on words here with SOULMATE, especially because the crosses yield at least S_U____E pretty readily. 

57A [Pioneering P.D.A.] — Such a straightforward clue and feels like a lost opportunity to make a play on PALM PILOT. 

59A [Soft drink originally made with egg whites] — A neat factoid but surely you didn’t need to tell us “Soft drink” which gives it away immediately (SODA is obvious and then you only need one cross to get CREAM)

I assume that these were the result of the editors trying to make this more accessible (or maybe, as @XWordJunkie speculates, overcompensating for the TARANA/SCHEMA crossing — which, since the clue for 24 points to SCHEMA much more than SCHEME, seemed pretty standard to me).

But then the others were guesses, too, and since I can't run letters like those who solve online, I was pleased that all my guesses turned out to be correct without having to look anything up.

One thing I don't understand: Why was Ship back clued with a question mark? Seemed like a totally straightforward clue to me. Maybe because I've been on a lot of cruise and buy on Amazon only when absolutely necessary?

There was the experience of the NW corner, where I had heaps of trouble, and then there was everything else where I found the puzzle quite solvable.  But one of the reasons I had so much trouble in the NW is that I misread 3D as "small sNack" for almost the entirety of my solve.  And I wanted NOSH -- the only 4-letter word to fit that clue.  But no letters fit -- that H was completely unworkable -- so I left the whole area empty, other than EAT and ACAI.  

I needed to cheat on TARANA to finish that section.  Aha.  It's SCHEMA, not SCHEME!  I wouldn't have thought to correct that.  

I had the P from GOD HELP ME and tried to make the "pioneering PDA" a PHONE of some sort.  This dovetailed nicely with IN RE for the "list header."  Alas, it was PILOT and TO DO -- so my SE corner is a hot mess of writeovers.

It is quite remarkable how many G-O-T Starks I don't know.  How many don't I know?  All of them!!!

I didn't know the Wilde quote, but had enough crosses to piece together LIFE IMITATES ART on my own.  This was the most important entry for using my lower sections of the puzzle prowess to help solve my NW  befuddlement.  Oh, and now I see that 3D has to be PECK, not NOSH.  And now I see "small sMack" instead of "small sNack".

Thought for the day:  With a tagline like that, I would move heaven and earth to avoid seeing BACKDRAFT.  Not even a trailer for BACKDRAFT.  GOD HELP ME, but that movie sounds truly awful!

Something about life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness, dissolving political bands, tyranny and despotism, unalienable rights endowed by our Creator (not the State, sorry commies), consent of the governed, etc., etc.  ...I must have my dates mixed up.

I found it just crunchy enough to make me pause; OCEANARIA/TARANA being one set of unknowns, but plugging away at the crossings gave me my reward. I’m chuffed I got the galaxy as Space isn’t generally my forte. 

Love the TELEX reminder. In one of my first office jobs in the late 70’s, telexing overseas customers was one of my roles. It was becoming obsolete even then, so was banished to an otherwise unused floor in a very old building. In the Winter I needed fingerless gloves to type with, as they didn’t bother to heat that floor. There was also a massive Gastetner (not sure if I’ve spelled that properly) printer up there. I wasn’t trusted to operate that. Just as well as it was the size of a small car and I would probably have just covered myself in ink. Fortunately the company had invested in a newfangled machine called the fax.

After a smooth solve except for the upper left where I had to take out a bunch of stuff before it fell back together, I got intrigued by the information on XWStats.

Over the last year, my averages are substantially below my All Time averages. But over the last 60 days, my Saturday averages have jumped back up to meet my All Time Saturday average. 

This appears to be due to two outliers. Seven solves are within a few minutes of each other, clustering close to or even slightly below my 1-year average. The two outliers are each more than 3x that.

I see a similar trend for Thursdays and Sundays, with one and two outliers, respectively, over the past 60 days.

I'm having fun coming up with theories about this.

This was a terrific tough Saturday puzzle that slowed me down, but not too badly. It had a few great clues, my favorite being "Rock instrument?" for STONE TOOL. At the end I could not see that SCHEMe should be SCHEMA (never having heard of TARANA),so cheated on the last letter. (What constitutes cheating is entirely up to each solver, of course.)

At the end I learned a new word: VENDEE for buyer. (Must come from the French "vendée" meaning "sold", right?) But I didn't like the word ADORE being clued with "Fancy", because their meanings seem quite disparate to me. Fancy means to like or to want, whereas adore means to like tremendously (and carries no sense of to want.)

In case you didn't do the Thursday puzzle, the NYT DID NOT IGNORE the July 4th holiday.  

They just published the holiday puzzle a bit early so they could make it an absolutely gorgeous Thursday.

So it's not an anti-American conspiracy by the NYT to disparage our fair land.  I'm particularly sensitive to that comment, because I consider our country to be (to have been?) a work in progress, with many fine accomplishments, but with many problems that it desperately needs to work out.

People like me are often attacked for speaking badly of the country.  The full situation is this:  when necessary, I criticized my kids as they were growing up.  Not because I didn't love them, but because I did, and I knew that unless they rectified their behavior (and we all needed rectified behavior along the way) they were going to suffer for it in the future.

For example, I am now of the opinion that it would have been far better for the founding fathers to not do it--they should never have founded a half-free, half-slave nation.  It should have been obvious it would never work, and we have suffered ever since.  

I think the US had the possibility of becoming an even more exceptionally strong and long-lasting country, but only if it had overcome the matter of slavery before it was founded, not after.

To paraphrase William F. Buckley, the founding fathers should have "[stood] athwart history, yelling Stop".

Is a puzzle never easier than its most difficult cross?  Or is the difficulty some kind of "average" difficulty of all the entries?

When posed in this way, I find this easy puzzle to have been impossible.  Feels like a paradox until we start to really think about the terms we're using.

When I posed the shamelessly name Francis Conjecture, I made sure to include that it *had* to be a complete, clean solve, which I think implies the former--any single cross can make a crossword difficult.

Here is my check list for flyspecking.  

First, if there are rebuses, re-enter them carefully.

Second, re-evaluate all vowels in every entry.  So many times it's something like today, and "E" for an "A", or yesterday I don't know how many ways I spelled TEMPERA, esp the second "E".  (Had I done this today, I might have found SCHEMA/SCHEMe.)

Third, look for an accidental 0 instead of an O.  Very hard to discern.  I often do a page search (I'm on a browser) for "0" to make sure it only highlights actual zeroes.

Fourth, check all "C"'s and "K"'s.  You only have to die on EKG/ECG or ORKA/ORCA a couple of times to watch for this one.

Beyond that it's really tough.  Maybe an "S" for a "Z" as in Les Mis(z).
